components.name: AboutComponent

propertiesClass.name: bulletPoints
propertiesClass.defaultValue:
[
    'Open source and free to use, for state and federal planners, as well as the public',
    'Supports regional planning for fire resilience and ecological benefits across broader landscapes',
    'Utilizes the latest Regional Resource Kits as the primary data source',
    'Incorporates the best state and federal science and models',
  ]

components.templateData:
Planscape is a new wildfire resilience planning tool

Designed to bring the best available state & federal data and science together, Planscape guides regional planners in identifying the optimal landscapes to treat in order to mitigate fire risk, maximize ecological benefits, and help California's landscapes adapt to climate change.

  • {{ bullet }}

The data used is based on California's Regional Resource Kits, developed by the California Wildfire & Forest Resilience Task Force. You can find more detailed information about these here.

import { Component, Input, Output, EventEmitter } from '@angular/core'; import type { User } from './User'; @Component({ selector: 'storybook-header', template: `

Acme

Welcome, {{ user.name }}!
`, // styleUrls: ['./header.css'], }) export default class HeaderComponent { @Input() user: User | null = null; @Output() onLogin = new EventEmitter(); @Output() onLogout = new EventEmitter(); @Output() onCreateAccount = new EventEmitter(); }

components.name: PageComponent
components.template:
Pages in Storybook

We recommend building UIs with a component-driven process starting with atomic components and ending with pages.

Render pages with mock data. This makes it easy to build and review page states without needing to navigate to them in your app. Here are some handy patterns for managing page data in Storybook:

  • Use a higher-level connected component. Storybook helps you compose such data from the "args" of child component stories
  • Assemble data in the page component from your services. You can mock these services out using Storybook.

Get a guided tutorial on component-driven development at Storybook tutorials . Read more in the docs .

Tip Adjust the width of the canvas with the Viewports addon in the toolbar
components.sourceCode:
import { Component } from '@angular/core'; import type { User } from './User'; @Component({ selector: 'storybook-page', template: `

Pages in Storybook

We recommend building UIs with a component-driven process starting with atomic components and ending with pages.

Render pages with mock data. This makes it easy to build and review page states without needing to navigate to them in your app. Here are some handy patterns for managing page data in Storybook:

  • Use a higher-level connected component. Storybook helps you compose such data from the "args" of child component stories
  • Assemble data in the page component from your services. You can mock these services out using Storybook.

Get a guided tutorial on component-driven development at Storybook tutorials . Read more in the docs .

Tip Adjust the width of the canvas with the Viewports addon in the toolbar
`, // styleUrls: ['./page.css'], }) export default class PageComponent { user: User | null = null; doLogout() { this.user = null; } doLogin() { this.user = { name: 'Jane Doe' }; } doCreateAccount() { this.user = { name: 'Jane Doe' }; } }

components.name: PreviewComponent
components.templateData:
Welcome to Planscape preview!

What is Planscape?

Planscape is a new wildfire resilience planning support tool designed to bring the best available state & federal data and science together to enable better land management. Planscape is for you, the regional wildland planners, to help prioritize landscape treatments. With Planscape, you can mitigate fire risk, maximize ecological benefits, and help California's landscapes adapt to climate change.

Planscape includes all data from the Regional Resource Kit layers for the Sierra Nevada, Southern California, and Central Coast regions.

What can I do with Planscape?

You can view and explore the landscape resilience planning data on a set of maps. You can also visualize this data for the three regions alongside other provided data sets such as county and watershed boundaries.

Future Planscape releases will give you the ability to create plans, to identify treatment areas from the plans, and to collaborate with other stakeholders.

How do I learn more?

Click on the Help icon on the top right corner of the tool to read the user guide or check out the Planscape Release Notes or FAQ . You can also visit Planscape.org for more information about the tool. If you would like to receive our newsletter for regular updates, you can sign up here .

Conditions of Use

Please read our Conditions of Use for information regarding usage of this tool.

With a Planscape account, you can:

  • Save planning areas

  • Create plans, configurations, and scenarios

  • Download shapefiles & metadata

  • Share work with collaborators

As a guest user, you can:

  • Explore regional data on the map

  • View plans and/or scenarios that are publicly visible*

*Accessible only via direct links

components.name: WelcomeComponent
components.templateData:
Welcome to Planscape!

What is Planscape?

Planscape is a new wildfire resilience planning support tool designed to bring the best available state & federal data and science together to enable better land management. Planscape is for you, the regional wildland planners, to help prioritize landscape treatments. With Planscape, you can mitigate fire risk, maximize ecological benefits, and help California’s landscapes adapt to climate change.

Planscape includes all data from the Regional Resource Kit layers for the Sierra Nevada, Southern California, {{ showsNorthCal ? ' Northern California, ' : '' }} and Central Coast regions.

What can I do with Planscape?

You can view and explore the landscape resilience planning data on a set of maps. You can also visualize this data for the three regions alongside other provided data sets such as county and watershed boundaries.

Future Planscape releases aim to give you the ability to create and save planning areas, to create scenarios for various treatments, and to collaborate with other stakeholders.

Can Planscape actually help with planning?

Yes! A lot more functionality, including the ability to create, edit, save, and share plans is coming. The tool will also include integration with ForSys to allow planners to output projects that are optimized within provided constraints.

How do I do more?

Click on the Help icon on the top right corner of the tool to read the user guide or check out the Planscape Release Notes or FAQ . You can also visit Planscape.org for more information about the tool. If you would like to receive our newsletter for regular updates, you can sign up here .

How can I get started?

If you want a preview of the mapping tool, click Explore.

Explore

Already have an account? Click here to login or create an account.

Please read our Conditions of Use for information regarding usage of this tool.

variables.name: WINDOW
variables.description:
Window service This service provides a way of getting the window object via a dependency injection This allows to mock and provide the window object as well as applying best practices.

How it works? The main idea is to provide a token when using window instead of referencing window object directly.

Avoid this:

Example :
export class YourComponent {

  getAgent() {
    window.navigator.userAgent;
  }
}

Use this instead:

Example :
export class YourComponent {

 constructor(@Inject(WINDOW) private window: Window)

  getAgent() {
    this.window.navigator.userAgent;
  }
}

There are several ways of implementing this idea, this one is taken from https://brianflove.com/2018-01-11/angular-window-provider/

Testing

You can provide a mock of the window object when declaring your test module

await TestBed.configureTestingModule({ declarations: [YourComponent], providers: [ { provide: WINDOW, useValue: { YourWindowMock }, }, ], }).compileComponents();

And then get the window reference on your tests

Example :
const window = TestBed.inject(WINDOW);
